comment_content:
The course covers a wide range of cybersecurity topics. Some topics are covered in depth, while others are only briefly touched upon.
Topics that are covered are system security, hardware security, web security, network security, ML security, and LLM security.
comment_teaching: The professor seemed very interested and passionate about cybersecurity. However his teaching wasn't the best. Too many topics where covered without depth. The technologies and concepts were also badly taught.
comment_workload:
Some of the assignment were very hard. As a previous reviewer said, the first two assignments were very difficult and the rest weren't as difficult. Assignments and homework was given almost every week.
The midterm exam was very time-constrained but we had plenty of time during the final exam.
comment_grading:
There was a midterm, final exam, and lots of assignment.
The marks for all the assignments were released after the final exam so there was no way of knowing how well you had performed.

10% of the grade is based on in-class quiz and participation.

comment_content: The course covers a wide range of interesting cybersecurity topics. A large part of the course is based on the Stanford CS155 course (including coursework, and even some final exam questions). However, there is an interesting LLM security section at the end.
comment_teaching:
The professor has a good understanding of the topics he covered, however, he often assumed a high level of background knowledge and would go quickly through sections which could have done with further clarification.

The professor seemed to check the module's discussion board regularly enough, and students could get answers from him outside of class time.

I never had to interact with the teaching aids so I do not know how they are for the course.
comment_workload: The workload for this course is fairly heavy. It covers a lot of content and there are almost weekly tasks such as mini projects or problem sets which take up significant time. As mentioned in the grading section, the first few of these programming tasks are much harder than the rest and will likely take up a fairly significant amount of time. The later tasks are easier and quicker, but still take up some time each week – on top of often having to go over the large amount of content presented in the lectures.
comment_grading:
The grading was relatively generous. With some extra background knowledge in C and Go, I could relatively easily get high marks on the assignments, and the final exam was relatively easy. Without some understanding of C, expect to spend lots of time on mini-projects.

However, the first two projects were exceptionally hard compared to the rest of the course, which caused multiple people to drop the course. Additionally, the midterm exam was very time-constrained; however, it seemed the professor realised this, so the final exam had plenty of time.

Some extra marks were given in the first lecture for being the first to pick a lock. And a small percentage of the grade is based on participation; the lecturer would write down names when a question was answered, or for activity on the module discussion board.

The marks were also released very late so I could not tell before my final exam how well I had done in the rest of the course.

comment_content: Content is Interesting. Completely based off CS155 of Stanford with interesting LLM part
comment_teaching:
The professor clearly knows their stuff, but the way this course is run is a complete disaster. The lecturer assumes you already have a ton of background knowledge, which is ridiculous. They throw in a bunch of additional readings that are never even mentioned during class—what's the point? That being said, it's a good try for the first time but I wish the course planning would have kept UST students in mind and was not just copied of Stanford CS155 course.

To make matters worse, they didn’t even bother to release sample solutions for projects and assignments, leaving students in the dark about whether they’re getting things right or completely failing. In the end, taking this course felt like using a GPS with no signal—you're lost, confused, and the only thing you can count on is that you're definitely not where you need to be.

Given C only coz the prof is a chill guy
comment_workload: BADDDDD. The Projects made me question why I took CS. Okayish if you are referencing the solutions from the Stanford course (BUT I DONT THINK THAT SHOULD BE THE OBJECTIVE) otherwise insanely difficult (would rather do COMP3711 assignments again). And the TA is the most IRRELEVANT part of the course.
comment_grading:
Haven't released project grades :(
Hopefully can update..
